name: From Tolosa to the Larraun Valley — circular via the Araxes River
name_original: De Tolosa al Valle de Larraun — circular por el Río Araxes
region_intro: Road cycling around Tolosa offers diverse and picturesque landscapes. The region is characterized by its verdant Oria River valley, surrounded by lush green landscapes and the foothills of the Pyrenees mountains. This terrain provides varying degrees of challenging climbs and rewarding panoramic views, with numerous river valleys and mountain passes. Best road cycling routes around Tolosa The most popular road cycling route is The Basaburua minor Valley — circular from Tolosa, a 52.4 miles (84.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 24 minutes to complete. This difficult route navigates through scenic valleys and offers significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is From Tolosa to the Larraun Valley — circular via the Araxes River, a difficult 36.8 miles (59.3 km) path. This route follows the Araxes River, providing views of the surrounding green landscapes. Local road cyclists also love the Lizarrusti Pass – Otzaurte loop from Tolosa, a 67.5 miles (108.6 km) trail leading through mountain passes, often completed in about 4 hours 56 minutes. Road cycling around Tolosa is defined by green valleys, river valleys, and mountain passes in the foothills of the Pyrenees. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Tolosa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 20 reviews. More than 2600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Tolosa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Tolosa?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes around Tolosa, offering a variety of experiences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 20 reviews.

Q: What types of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Tolosa?
A: Tolosa's road cycling routes feature diverse terrain, from the verdant Oria River valley to the foothills of the Pyrenees. You'll encounter challenging climbs, rewarding panoramic views, and pleasant rides along river banks. The region is known for its mountainous terrain, green valleys, and notable passes like Lizarrusti Pass and Alto de Iturburu.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Tolosa?
A: While many routes in Tolosa are classified as moderate to difficult, the region does offer options suitable for varying fitness levels. The Plazaola Greenway, though not exclusively a road cycling route, is a paved path suitable for all skill levels, including family outings, following an old railway line for 37 kilometers. For road cycling specifically, routes with minimal elevation gain are available, though they might still require some basic fitness.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes in the area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Tolosa offers several difficult routes with significant elevation gain. Notable options include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1032759734/the-basaburua-minor-valley-circular-from-tolosa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Basaburua minor Valley — circular from Tolosa&lt;/a&gt;, a 84.4 km route with substantial climbing, and the 108.6 km &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5347089" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lizarrusti Pass – Otzaurte loop from Tolosa&lt;/a&gt;, which navigates through mountain passes.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the road cycling routes in Tolosa?
A: Tolosa's routes offer access to stunning natural features. You can cycle past the protected &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1813687" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Leitzaran River Valley&lt;/a&gt;, known for its clean waters and riverside forests. Mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3083575" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lizarrusti Pass&lt;/a&gt; provide breathtaking views, and some routes offer glimpses of peaks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1016277" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Txindoki&lt;/a&gt;. You might even encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3049380" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Amezketa Stream Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; on certain paths.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available from Tolosa?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Tolosa are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1032759734/the-basaburua-minor-valley-circular-from-tolosa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Basaburua minor Valley — circular from Tolosa&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1035201859/from-tolosa-to-the-larraun-valley-circular-via-the-araxes-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Tolosa to the Larraun Valley — circular via the Araxes River&lt;/a&gt;, which follows the scenic Araxes River.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Tolosa?
A: The region's lush green landscapes suggest that spring and autumn offer ideal conditions for road cycling, with pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer can also be excellent, though some climbs might be more challenging in warmer weather. Winter cycling is possible, but routes at higher elevations might be affected by colder temperatures or occasional snow.

Q: What kind of cycling infrastructure and support can I find in Tolosa?
A: Tolosa and the wider Tolosaldea region actively promote cycling. The Usabal Sports Centre in Tolosa houses a Mountain Bike Centre, which offers technical cycling services. The area also features additional bike paths and is a frequent host for major cycling events, indicating a supportive environment for cyclists.

Q: Are there any specific passes or climbs that are popular with road cyclists in Tolosa?
A: Yes, the region is well-known for several popular climbs. Cyclists frequently tackle passes such as Lizarrusti Pass, Alto de Iturburu, Larraitz, Alkiza, and Altzo. Alto de Iturburu has even been featured in editions of the San Sebastian Classic cycling race, highlighting its reputation among serious cyclists.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Tolosa?
A: The road cycling routes in Tolosa are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an impressive average rating of 4.9 stars. Cyclists often praise the diverse and picturesque landscapes, the varying degrees of challenging climbs, and the rewarding panoramic views of the Tolosaldea region.

Q: Can I find routes that offer views of significant landmarks or peaks?
A: Absolutely. Many routes provide stunning views of the surrounding mountains and landmarks. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24943139" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Txindoki from Ordizia – Kaxeta loop from Tolosa&lt;/a&gt; offers excellent vistas of the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1016277" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Txindoki&lt;/a&gt; peak. Other routes will take you near summits like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839675" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Uzturre Summit&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3569577" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hernio Summit&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there options for road cycling that incorporate river valleys?
A: Yes, Tolosa is situated in the Oria River valley, and many routes follow river courses. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1035201859/from-tolosa-to-the-larraun-valley-circular-via-the-araxes-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Tolosa to the Larraun Valley — circular via the Araxes River&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering scenic views along the Araxes River. The Leitzaran River Valley is also a protected biotope with clean waters and riverside forests, providing a beautiful setting for cycling.

The Alto de Azpirotz is a mountain pass that is better known for its geographical position than for the hardness or percentages of the ascent. Basically since it is on the border of the province of Guipúzcoa, where it is on the edge of the Valle de Larraun.

Berrobi is a typical town of these northern regions of the Empty Country and its name Berrobi comes from watercress and has various meanings in the Basque language. All of them generally related to humid and leafy vegetation.
